text: sharing a splenic flexure resection for a large splenic flexure tumor. You can see it here on the CAT scan. This is my port setup. It was not clear going in whether or not there was T for invasion into the spleen. So I'm taking a look to start. And although there's some adhesions to the spleen that did not look secondary to cancer.
The laparoscopic camera is positioned in the super pubic port, providing a view straight up. The surgeon performs a sub-IMV dissection along the DJ flexure, manipulating the tissue in this area.
The surgeon encounters difficulty due to a tattoo located in the retroperitoneum, which obscures the view of the pancreas. The surgeon proceeds to take the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV).

The surgeon moves the camera to port two and continues the sub-IMV dissection, tracing the ureter towards the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA). The dissection progresses upwards to locate the ascending left colic branch emerging from the IMA.

The surgeon isolates the ascending left colic branch approximately two centimeters from the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), visible through the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV). The area is heavily tattooed, obscuring the pancreas from the inferior medial view.

The surgeon continues the medial dissection, focusing on the area around the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) and ascending left colic branch, with the camera positioned to provide a clear view of the dissection field.

The surgeon tucks a sponge into the surgical field and performs lateral dissection, taking a portion of the distal mesentery near the left colic branch. The dissection progresses into the medial plane towards the spinal collection, attempting to separate the splenic flexure from the spleen. The pancreas remains obscured from this angle, prompting a shift to a supramesocolic approach.
The surgeon separates the omentum from the colon at the planned proximal margin, entering into the lesser sac.

The surgeon carefully separates the omentum from the greater curvature of the stomach, encountering what appear to be inflammatory adhesions. Frozen sections are sent for analysis, which return negative results for tumor invasion.
The surgeon attempts to create space to separate the splenic flexure from the spleen, continuing the dissection without a clear view of the pancreas from the current angle.

The surgeon makes progress in the dissection, identifying the pancreas in the upper field. The dissection continues with alternating lateral and supermusicalic approaches to improve visibility and access.
The surgeon attempts to separate the transverse colon mesentery from the pancreas, which appears fused. The dissection follows closely along the base of the pancreas to achieve separation.

The surgeon approaches laterally to separate the remaining tissue from the tail of the pancreas, revealing the colon clearly. With the pancreas out of the way, the surgeon safely dissects the last portion of the lineocolic ligament.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of the specimen, selecting the proximal margin and creating a window on either side of the left branch of the middle colic artery. The proximal mesentery is taken up to the bowel wall, followed by the distal mesentery on the downside of the left branch of the ascending left colic artery. The specimen is then divided.

The surgeon positions the specimen above the liver and examines the dissection area, revealing the renal vein and tail of the pancreas. There is visible ischemia from taking an inferior pole branch of the spleen. The surgeon performs a side-to-side functional end-to-end anastomosis using a V-Loc suture, joining the posterior staple lines. Colotomies are made, and ICG is used to confirm perfusion of the inner lip.

The surgeon uses an inner 3-0 V-Loc suture to perform an anastomosis, starting in the middle and sewing out and around the far side first. The second inner layer is sewn from the middle around the near side.
The surgeon brings two sutures to the middle and ties them together, completing a two-layer sewn anastomosis with an outer layer of Tuo PDS, securing it to the original stay suture.

The surgeon inspects the dissection area to ensure no further ischemia has occurred on the spleen, noting only a small tip is affected. The specimen is then extracted through the suprapubic 12mm port, which is upsized to accommodate the extraction.
